era_context: The late 17th century, dominated by European state consolidation and colonial competition. The Franco-Dutch War (1672–78) is concluding. The Popish Plot hysteria grips England. The Ottoman Empire faces increasing pressure from European powers. China's Kangxi Emperor is consolidating Qing control. Scientific revolution and early Enlightenment thought are spreading among European intellectuals.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian steppe and nomadic politics
Southeast Asian interior kingdoms (limited European documentation)

events.title: Treaty of Nijmegen — Signing and initial ratification
events.region: Dutch Republic, France, Spain, Holy Roman Empire, England
events.description: The Treaty of Nijmegen was signed in a series of bilateral treaties between August and September 1678, concluding the Franco-Dutch War (1672–78). France gained Franche-Comté from Spain and several towns in the Spanish Netherlands. The Dutch Republic retained its independence and most territories but ceded some gains to France. England, under Charles II, mediated and benefited from French subsidies. The treaty represented a major diplomatic achievement for French foreign minister Colbert de Croissy and marked France's emergence as the dominant European power. Ratification and implementation would continue into 1679.

sources.name: Treaty of Nijmegen, primary text (multiple language versions)
Diplomatic correspondence of European courts, August–September 1678
Colbert de Croissy's dispatches and reports

events.certainty_note: Treaty texts are precisely dated and extensively documented in multiple archives.

events.title: Popish Plot — Peak of hysteria and trials in England
events.description: The Popish Plot, a fabricated conspiracy allegedly orchestrated by Catholic Jesuits to assassinate King Charles II and restore Catholicism to England, reached its peak in 1678. Informant Titus Oates presented his accusations to Parliament and the Privy Council in September 1678. The plot triggered widespread panic, anti-Catholic violence, and a series of trials and executions of alleged conspirators (most notably the execution of William Stafford in December 1678). The hysteria lasted until 1681 and profoundly influenced English politics, strengthening Whig efforts to exclude the Catholic Duke of York from succession. Modern scholarship confirms the plot was entirely fabricated.
key_figures: Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury (Whig leader exploiting the plot)
events.certainty_note: Events are precisely dated and extensively documented. Modern scholarship has definitively established the plot as fabricated.
cross_references: 1679-002 (Habeas Corpus Act, response to Popish Plot fears)

events.title: Scanian War — Major battles and Swedish military setbacks
events.description: The Scanian War (1675–79) continued with major battles in 1678, including the Battle of Lund (December 1676, but with continued operations in 1678) and the Battle of Malmö (1677–78). Swedish forces under King Charles XI faced a coalition of Denmark-Norway and Brandenburg. Despite initial Swedish victories, the war became increasingly costly and inconclusive. By 1678, both sides were exhausted and seeking peace, leading to negotiations that would culminate in the Peace of Lund (1679). The war demonstrated the limits of Swedish military power and the rising strength of Brandenburg.

sources.name: Swedish military records and battle reports

events.certainty_note: Military records and battle accounts are well-documented in Scandinavian archives.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor — Consolidation of power after Oboi regency
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor, who had assumed personal rule in 1667 after the death of regent Oboi, continued consolidating his authority in 1678. He faced the ongoing Revolt of the Three Feudatories (1673–81), which threatened Qing control of southern China. Kangxi's strategy combined military pressure with diplomatic overtures and administrative reforms. In 1678, he was strengthening his position for the final suppression of the revolt. His reign marked a transition from the early Qing period of conquest to a period of cultural and administrative consolidation.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)

events.certainty_note: Imperial records and military documentation confirm Kangxi's consolidation efforts in 1678.

events.title: Pueblo Revolt — Spanish expulsion from New Mexico
events.region: New Mexico, Spanish colonial North America
events.description: In August 1680, the Pueblo Revolt erupted, but the immediate aftermath and Spanish response began in 1678–79 as Spanish authorities in Mexico City received reports of unrest and began investigating the causes of the revolt. Governor Antonio de Otermín, who would lead the Spanish forces during the actual revolt in 1680, was already in position. The period 1678–79 saw increasing tensions between Spanish colonial authorities and Pueblo peoples, particularly over religious suppression and labor demands. Spanish sources from this period document growing awareness of discontent.

sources.name: Spanish colonial documents, Archivo General de Indias

events.certainty_note: Spanish documentation is detailed; the exact timing of Pueblo planning is less well-documented in surviving sources.

events.title: Ottoman-Russian relations — Russo-Turkish War aftermath
events.region: Ottoman Empire, Russian Tsardom, Black Sea region
events.description: Following the Russo-Turkish War (1676–81), Ottoman-Russian relations remained tense in 1678. The war had begun over control of the Dnieper region and Cossack territories. By 1678, both powers were engaged in military operations and diplomatic maneuvering. The Ottoman Empire, under Sultan Mehmed IV, sought to maintain its influence in the Black Sea region and over the Crimean Khanate. Russia, under Tsar Feodor III, was attempting to expand southward. The conflict would continue until 1681, when the Treaty of Bakhchisaray would establish a temporary peace.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman and Russian records document the conflict; exact dating of specific events in 1678 is sometimes approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The Popish Plot is now universally recognized by historians as entirely fabricated, yet it had profound real-world consequences. This illustrates how false narratives can drive historical events. The plot's impact on English politics was genuine even though the plot itself was not. Additionally, the significance of the Treaty of Nijmegen is sometimes overstated as a 'balance of power' settlement; modern scholarship emphasizes that it actually represented a decisive French victory and the establishment of French hegemony, not a balanced settlement. The Kangxi Emperor's consolidation is sometimes portrayed as inevitable; recent scholarship notes significant resistance and the possibility of Qing fragmentation had the Three Feudatories revolt succeeded.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1678 is rich for European and East Asian events, with extensive state archives and diplomatic records. European sources are predominantly elite and court-centric. The Popish Plot is exceptionally well-documented due to its political importance and the survival of trial records, parliamentary debates, and contemporary printed accounts. Chinese sources benefit from Confucian emphasis on record-keeping but are similarly court-centric. Ottoman and Russian sources are less abundant in Western archives but are preserved in Istanbul and Moscow. Colonial North American sources are heavily Spanish-centric; Indigenous perspectives are largely absent. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific Island, and Southeast Asian events are largely absent from surviving European-language documentation.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Nijmegen signing in 1678 led to ratification and implementation in 1679.
Popish Plot hysteria in 1678 directly motivated the Habeas Corpus Act of 1679.
Popish Plot hysteria contributed to the Exclusion Crisis of 1681.
Military exhaustion in Scanian War led to Peace of Lund in 1679.
Kangxi's consolidation efforts in 1678 culminated in suppression of Three Feudatories revolt in 1681.
